rationale: Art.21(1) requires proportionate measures relative to entity size, risk exposure and incident likelihood, while ISO 27005 Cl.5 (Information security risk management overview) establishes the iterative risk management framework within which proportionality decisions are made but does not itself mandate proportionality as a binding criterion.

Art.21(1) mandates an all-hazards approach covering network, information systems and physical environment; ISO 27005 Cl.6.1 (Organizational considerations) scopes the risk management context including external/internal issues and ISMS scope, but is information-security-centric rather than explicitly all-hazards.

Art.21(1) requires measures to account for state-of-the-art and European/international standards; ISO 27005 Cl.6.2 (Identifying basic requirements of interested parties) requires capturing regulatory, legal and contractual requirements as risk criteria inputs, which is a necessary but not sufficient mechanism for operationalising a state-of-the-art obligation.

Art.21(1) instructs entities to set a level of security appropriate to the risks, taking into account exposure, size and likelihood/severity; ISO 27005 Cl.6.3 (Establishing and maintaining information security risk criteria) directly operationalises this by requiring definition of risk acceptance criteria and criteria for performing risk assessments.

Art.21(2)(a) requires a documented policy governing how risk analysis is performed; ISO 27005 Cl.5 defines the overarching information security risk management process framework that such a policy must describe, making the NIS2 policy requirement a governance subset of the ISO process model.

Art.21(2)(a) mandates a policy that governs risk analysis scope and methodology; ISO 27005 Cl.6 (Context establishment) covers the prerequisite inputs (organisational context, interested-party requirements, risk criteria) that a NIS2-compliant risk-analysis policy must address.

Art.21(2)(a) requires that risk analysis be performed as part of a policy-driven process; ISO 27005 Cl.7 (Information security risk assessment process) directly describes the execution of risk identification, analysis and evaluation — a direct 1-to-1 functional equivalence at process level.

Art.21(2)(a) implicitly requires risk identification as a component of risk analysis; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 (confirmed by PECB/Scribd sources to cover risk identification with event-based and asset-based approaches) is the direct process step — clause number unverified against official text. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

Art.21(1) instructs that proportionality account for likelihood and severity; Art.21(2)(a) requires risk analysis policy address same; ISO 27005 Cl.7.3 (risk analysis — likelihood and consequence estimation) is the direct process step but exact sub-clause number unverified against official text. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.3 'Analysing information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

Art.21(2)(a) policy on risk analysis implicitly requires a risk evaluation step to decide on treatment; ISO 27005 Cl.7.4 (risk evaluation — comparing analysis results against risk criteria) operationalises this — clause number unverified against official text. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.4 'Evaluating the information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

Art.21(2)(a) requires policies on information system security as part of risk management; ISO 27005 Cl.6.1 addresses organisational context including establishing the ISMS scope, but does not itself mandate creation of an information-system-security policy (that is an ISO 27001 Cl.5.2 obligation).
Art.21(2)(a) compliance requires documented evidence of the risk analysis; ISO 27005 Cl.10.4.3 (Documented information about results) directly mandates retention of risk assessment outputs.

Art.21(2)(a) requires a policy defining how risk analysis is conducted; ISO 27005 Cl.10.4.2 (Documented information about processes) requires maintaining documented information describing the risk management process.

Art.21(2)(f) requires policies and procedures to assess effectiveness of cybersecurity measures; ISO 27005 Cl.5 establishes the overall iterative risk management cycle of which effectiveness assessment is the monitoring/review feedback loop.

CIR 2024/2690 Section 7.1 requires entities to determine what measures are to be monitored/measured; ISO 27005 Cl.9.1 (Monitoring, measurement, analysis and evaluation — per ISO 27001-aligned structure) covers exactly this obligation — exact sub-clause number for 27005 unverified. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.1 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

CIR 2024/2690 Section 7.1 requires entities to determine measurement methods to ensure valid results; ISO 27005 Cl.9.1 covers methods for monitoring and measuring effectiveness of the risk management process — exact sub-clause number unverified. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.1 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

CIR 2024/2690 Section 7.1 requires specifying when monitoring and measuring is to be performed; ISO 27005 Cl.9.1 includes timing/frequency of monitoring and review activities — exact sub-clause number unverified. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.1 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

CIR 2024/2690 Section 7.1 requires designation of responsibility for monitoring effectiveness; ISO 27005 Cl.10.1 (Leveraging related ISMS processes — operation) addresses integration with ISMS responsibilities, but explicit accountability assignment is primarily an ISO 27001 Cl.5.3 obligation rather than a 27005 process step — clause number unverified. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.10.1 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.2 'Leadership and commitment'.]

CIR 2024/2690 Section 7.2 requires independent reviews at planned intervals and after significant incidents; ISO 27005 Cl.9.2 (internal audit of the risk management process — aligned to ISO 27001 structure) covers periodic review but 27005 does not explicitly mandate independence — clause number unverified. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.2 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

CIR 2024/2690 requires that effectiveness assessment results feed back into risk treatment prioritisation; ISO 27005 Cl.8 (Information security risk treatment process) includes identification and selection of treatment options informed by risk assessment, but the feedback loop from effectiveness measurement is only implicit in ISO 27005 rather than explicitly required.

Art.21(2)(f) effectiveness assessment must cover whether residual risk remains at acceptable levels post-treatment; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6.3 (Acceptance of the residual information security risk) directly governs the formal acceptance decision for residual risk.

Art.21(4) requires corrective measures when effectiveness assessment reveals non-compliance; ISO 27005 Cl.10.7 (Corrective action) formalises the requirement to analyse identified deficiencies and implement corrective actions.

Art.21(1) mandates a broad risk management framework covering all hazards (physical, cyber, operational) for network and information systems; ISO 27005 Cl.5 defines an information-security-specific risk management process, making NIS2 the broader superset obligation that ISO 27005 partially satisfies.

Art.21(1) implies ongoing/continuous risk management to maintain appropriate security levels; ISO 27005 Cl.9.3 (management review — aligned to ISO 27001 Cl.9.3 structure) covers periodic management review of the risk management programme — clause number for 27005 unverified. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.3 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

Art.21(2)(a) policy on risk analysis must define criteria used to evaluate risks (e.g., what constitutes acceptable risk for a critical infrastructure operator); ISO 27005 Cl.6.3 directly requires establishing and maintaining risk criteria including risk acceptance criteria and criteria for performing risk assessments.

Art.21(2)(a) policy must define the scope of risk analysis covering network/information systems; ISO 27005 Cl.6.1 (Organizational considerations) explicitly requires defining the scope of the information security risk management process aligned with the ISMS scope.

Art.21(2)(a) risk analysis policy must account for regulatory requirements (e.g., sector-specific obligations) and stakeholder expectations; ISO 27005 Cl.6.2 (Identifying basic requirements of interested parties) requires capturing legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements as inputs to risk criteria.

NIS2 all-hazards risk analysis requires identifying threat events and their impact on NIS; ISO 27005:2022 Annex A.2.4 (Event-based approach) provides the methodological guidance for identifying risks based on threat scenarios and events.

NIS2 risk analysis for network and information systems inherently requires asset identification (systems, services, data); ISO 27005:2022 Annex A.2.5 (Asset-based approach) provides the methodology for identifying risks based on specific assets and their vulnerabilities.

NIS2 risk analysis must address sequences of events from threats to impact on NIS security; ISO 27005:2022 Annex A.2.3 (Risk sources and desired end state) and the new risk scenario concept cover exactly this sequence-of-events modelling approach.

Art.21(2)(a) policy on risk analysis must define the criteria used to assess risks; ISO 27005:2022 Annex A.1.1 (Criteria related to risk assessment) provides guidance on establishing those criteria including consequence, likelihood, and risk level scales.

Art.21(2)(a) risk analysis policy must specify what level of risk is acceptable for the entity; ISO 27005:2022 Annex A.1.2 (Risk acceptance criteria) directly provides guidance on defining risk acceptance thresholds.

Art.21(1) explicitly extends the all-hazards scope to include physical environment of NIS systems; ISO 27005 Cl.6.1 addresses context establishment for information security risk management but its physical scope is limited — physical security risks are in scope of ISO 27005 only as they affect information assets.

Art.21(2)(f) policies for effectiveness assessment must include monitoring of the risk environment; ISO 27005:2022 Annex A.2.7 (Monitoring risk-related events) provides specific methodological guidance on how to monitor events that may affect the risk landscape.

Art.21(2)(f) effectiveness assessment results must inform management and relevant stakeholders to enable corrective action; ISO 27005 Cl.10.2 (communication and consultation — aligned with ISO 31000 structure) covers communication of risk information to stakeholders — exact sub-clause number for 27005:2022 unverified. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.10.2 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.3 'Communication and consultation'.]

Art.21(2)(a) policy on risk analysis implicitly requires that identified risks be addressed via a treatment plan; ISO 27005 Cl.8 (Information security risk treatment process) covers the complete treatment lifecycle including option selection, plan creation, and risk owner approval — making NIS2's implicit treatment requirement a subset of the full ISO 27005 Cl.8 process.

Art.21(2)(a) risk analysis policy must address selection of appropriate security measures (controls) to treat identified risks; ISO 27005 Cl.8.3 (Information security risk treatment — aligned sub-clause for treatment option selection) covers the process of identifying, selecting and planning risk treatment options — exact sub-clause number unverified. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 8.3 'Determining all controls necessary to implement the risk treatment options' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

Art.21(2)(a) requires that the risk management policy result in actionable treatment plans; ISO 27005 Cl.8.4 (risk treatment plan formulation — unverified sub-clause) covers the formal documentation of treatment plans including timelines and responsibilities. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.8.4 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 8.6 'Information security risk treatment plan'.]

Art.21(2)(a) risk analysis policy effectiveness requires management accountability for risk decisions; ISO 27005 Cl.8.5 (risk owner approval of treatment plan and acceptance of residual risk — unverified sub-clause) addresses formal risk owner sign-off, which is implied but not explicitly stated in NIS2 text. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.8.5 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 8.6 'Information security risk treatment plan'.]

Art.21(1) all-hazards scope implicitly includes third-party and supply-chain risks that affect NIS security; ISO 27005 Cl.6.1 context establishment includes identifying internal and external issues including supply-chain dependencies, though supply-chain risk is treated only insofar as it affects information assets.

Art.21(2)(a) policy on risk analysis implicitly requires that risk analysis be repeated periodically and after significant changes; ISO 27005 Cl.9.1 monitoring and review process includes triggers for re-initiating risk assessment — exact sub-clause number for 27005 unverified. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.1 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]
